coredata.dc:title: Organic complexing groups and processes for the selective recovery of lithium from lithium-ion battery leachates: A review
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Liberati, Ariella F.
Prelot, Benedicte
Mericq, Jean-Pierre
Faur, Catherine
Monge, Sophie
coredata.dc:description: This review aims at shedding some light on promising solutions for the recycling of lithium from wasted lithium-ion batteries. The use of Lithium-selective inorganic materials is described but is limited by synthesis and stability issues (energy-consuming and high-temperature synthesis, and degradation observed at low pH). Organic molecules that are capable of both selectively and efficiently interacting, as well as bonding with lithium ions have been abundantly reported in the literature over the past decades. They have various advantages such as mild-condition synthesis, great adaptability and easy processing. This manuscript highlights the numerous lithium-selective organic complexing groups that are reported in the literature, as well as different process designs in which they can be implemented. Firstly, the pre-treatment of used lithium-ion batteries and production of leachates is described. A study of the complexation selectivity and efficiency of organic complexing groups as well as complexation mechanisms and influence of experimental conditions are reported. Then, a comparison of different process designs for the recovery of lithium from aqueous solutions is given. Finally, decomplexation, recovery and reuse of the complexing materials are described along with the perspectives of promising opportunities for the future of the recycling industry.

head.ce:title: Organic complexing groups and processes for the selective recovery of lithium from lithium-ion battery leachates: A review

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: ICGM, Univ Montpellier, CNRS, ENSCM, Montpellier, France
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: ICGM, Univ Montpellier, CNRS, ENSCM, Montpellier, France

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: IEM, Univ Montpellier, CNRS, ENSCM, Montpellier, France
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: IEM, Univ Montpellier, CNRS, ENSCM, Montpellier, France

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This review aims at shedding some light on promising solutions for the recycling of lithium from wasted lithium-ion batteries. The use of Lithium-selective inorganic materials is described but is limited by synthesis and stability issues (energy-consuming and high-temperature synthesis, and degradation observed at low pH). Organic molecules that are capable of both selectively and efficiently interacting, as well as bonding with lithium ions have been abundantly reported in the literature over the past decades. They have various advantages such as mild-condition synthesis, great adaptability and easy processing. This manuscript highlights the numerous lithium-selective organic complexing groups that are reported in the literature, as well as different process designs in which they can be implemented. Firstly, the pre-treatment of used lithium-ion batteries and production of leachates is described. A study of the complexation selectivity and efficiency of organic complexing groups as well as complexation mechanisms and influence of experimental conditions are reported. Then, a comparison of different process designs for the recovery of lithium from aqueous solutions is given. Finally, decomplexation, recovery and reuse of the complexing materials are described along with the perspectives of promising opportunities for the future of the recycling industry.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Non-renewable resources have become drastically scarce because of their unlimited consumption for industrial applications. Among these non-renewable resources, the lightest existing metal, lithium, is currently exploited for many applications: glass and ceramics, batteries, lubricants, etc. [1]. It can be extracted from two main natural resources: in the form of ores as lithium carbonate (spodumene, amblygonite, jadarite, hectorite [2]) or as an ion in salt-lake brines [1]. In 2021, the US Geological Survey estimated that, to this day, 95 % of the production of lithium in the world is provided by Chile, Australia, China and Argentina. The global production of lithium has more than quadrupled over the past decade, by increasing from 35,000 t of produced lithium in 2012 to 146,000 t in 2022, and is expected to reach 1.25 Mt. by 2028. This is mainly due to the recent boom associated with lithium-ion batteries (60 % of total consumption in 2018) and their use in the electric vehicle (EV) industry. Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are prevalent on the reusable batteries market as they have the advantage of providing a high specific capacity, high energy density and suitable cycling stability [3]. Yet, one major downside is their recyclability: LIBs have a lifespan of 3 to 10 years and it was estimated that 200 to 500 million tons of spent LIB waste was generated annually by 2020 [4]. Since lithium resources are note unlimited, and their extractions remain problematic from a geopolitical and economical standpoint, developing industrially viable technologies to recycle lithium from spent lithium-ion batteries has evolved into a topic of great interest. Estimated at 5 % in China in 2017 [5], the wasted LIB collecting rates are still quite scarce nowadays in the world as industrials and citizens are not quite educated in this sense. This is a limiting factor to an effective recycling of lithium in LIBs, but many countries are starting to face regulations that will strongly reinforce collecting rates. For instance, the European Commission decided that each country in the EU should collect at least 45 % of wasted LIBs in 2016. This figure should reach 65 % in 2025 and 70 % in 2030. Other limiting factors for the industrial recycling of wasted LIBs that are worth noticing are for instance the geopolitical situation, or the low purity of recycled lithium with the existing recycling technologies, which is insufficient for the repurposing of lithium into new batteries. Finally, it is worth noticing that the price of lithium experienced a 14-fold increase between 2020 and 2022 (from 5400 USD to 75,000 USD per ton [6]), followed by a recent important decrease (almost back to the same prices as 2020 today). This fluctuating trend, driven by geopolitical uncertainties, escalates the vulnerability of industries that rely on lithium (such as the EV industry). In this sense, diversifying towards other supply channels such as the recycling industry of wasted LIBs makes great sense [7].

LIBs are rechargeable batteries composed of two electrodes (one positively charged called cathode, and one negatively charged, called anode), a separator and an electrolyte. At least a dozen types of lithium-ion batteries are commercially used nowadays. They differ from each other in cathode or anode composition. The most common LIBs are “ICR” batteries, made of Lithium Cobalt Oxide (LiCoO2) cathodes and graphite (C6) anodes, but the cathodes can be composed of lithium‑sulfur (LiS batteries) or other lithium-metal oxides such as Lithium Iron Phosphate (LFP batteries), Lithium-ion Manganese Oxide (LMO batteries), Lithium-ion Manganese Iron Phosphate, Lithium Nickel Manganese Cobalt Oxides (NMC batteries), Lithium Nickel Cobalt Aluminum Oxides (NCA), etc. The anodes can also be made of Silicon or Lithium Titanium Oxide (LTO). The separator, a microporous polyolefin (polypropylene, polyethylene or mixtures of both) placed between the electrodes, has a key importance for the transport of the ions from one electrode to another. This transport is permitted by the presence of an organic liquid electrolyte composed of a lithium salt (LiPF6, LiBF4, LiClO4) in dimethyl carbonate, diethyl carbonate and/or ethyl methyl carbonate. The electrolyte can also be made of a charged polymer, typically a polyelectrolyte (Lithium-ion polymer batteries) [8,9].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pre-treatments steps prior to recycling processes
ce:section.ce:para:
The two main industrial methods currently employed for the recycling of spent LIBs consist of pyrometallurgy and hydrometallurgy (or combinations of both). Beforehand, a series of pre-treatment steps common to both methods are essential to optimize the recovery of the valuable metals. First, the batteries are discharged (to allow for the workers to manipulate safely) and dismantled as some large parts can be easily separated (for example the plastic container from the electrodes). The following steps can vary, and can be used at the same time or in various orders, depending on whether they will be followed by pyro or hydrometallurgy and according to economic or strategic choices. The other mechanical techniques involve crushing, sieving, magnetic separation (Fe), gravitational separation in water (for instance, plastics float in water, heavier particles can settle), etc. Thermal treatments can also be employed, by using low temperature calcination methods (T > 250 °C): they require the use of organic additives and ligands to break the interacting bonds between various pieces of the batteries, or also to calcinate any adhesives/glue used during assembling. This step is rather energy-consuming and polluting, but useful in the pyrometallurgy process, as it requires similar apparatus. Finally, mechanochemical treatments consist in crushing the lithium-metal cathode and using acidic solutions to disassemble the cathode material. Organic solvents such as dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), dimethylformamide (DMF) or dimethylacetamide (DMAc) can also be used to dissolve some parts. Among all the possibilities, we chose to illustrate the Dutta method [10] (Fig. 1
), that contains the most essential pre-treatment steps, but several other examples are described in the literature [11–14]. Here, a minimal amount of water and centrifuging at high-speed yield three categories of compounds: light plastic parts float while metallic sheets sink and cathodic materials are suspended. On the one hand, plastics and metallic parts are washed with water (suspended part of the washing water is added to the cathodic material suspension) and dried. On the other hand, cathodic material solution is allowed to settle, after which it is decanted and dried. The preparation of the cathodic material is very important as its state can have great influence on the leaching efficiency in the end. For instance, by crushing and sieving their cathodic material, Shin et al. prove that the smaller the particle size, the easier the dissolution and the better the leaching [15].

More generally, the pre-treatment steps allow for an approximate sorting of the different components of the LIBs, while two different types of mixtures are obtained: either a powder called cathodic material or a leachate, both being mixtures of metals that could not be separated during the pre-treatment steps (Li, Co, Ni, Mn, Cu, Al, etc.). The cathodic material is usually destined to be treated in the pyrometallurgy process, while aqueous solutions of metals (leachates) are typically treated in hydrometallurgy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lithium recycling processes from spent LIBs

ce:section.ce:para: Pyrometallurgy is an efficient separation process that is based on a thermal approach. After a mechanical pre-treatment, the cathode material undergoes a high-temperature treatment, potentially reaching up to 1700 °C. With the pyrometallurgy method, reducing agents are necessary to directly produce alloys and metal oxides that can then be selectively separated [16]. A great extent of literature is available on this technology [17–19] and notably a recent in-depth critical review on pyrometallurgical processes for the recycling of spent LIBs was published [20]. Nevertheless, the high-temperature conditions of the metallurgy process have been a great drawback for industrial applications as they are highly energy-consuming and polluting because of toxic gas emissions. Additionally, the specific recovery of lithium with the pyrometallurgy process is ineffective as lithium has a high affinity with oxygen and therefore it is slagged (lost with the waste). Windisch-Kern et al. provide laboratory-scale promising solutions to tackle these downsides by adapting an InduRed reactor to the pyrometallurgical recovery of lithium [21]. The final reactor notably allowed limited contact time and intensity between gaseous reaction products and the molten phase, leading to a better control of the slagging.

Hydrometallurgy is a process based on the use of aqueous solutions to selectively extract the metals one after the other. This method requires a series of steps after the pre-treatments, followed by cathode material leaching and metal separation from the leachate. Many extractions have been proposed to separate the valuable metals. As the composition of the leachate is very complicated, it is nearly impossible to only use one technique, but rather a combination of several: solvent extractions (with the help of an extractant and an organic phase), chemical precipitation (with precipitants such as OH− or C2O4 2−), electrochemical process (metal deposition using an electrical source), adsorption processes (ion exchange resins and other sorbents), direct regeneration of cathode active materials from the leachate (using oxalate coprecipitation and high-temperature solid-phase reaction, or sol-gel method (evaporation, thermal decomposition, grinding and calcination)) [22–25]. This is how hydrometallurgy provides more flexibility than pyrometallurgy, as the choice of the leaching and extractants can be adapted to the compound that is to be separated. Specific processing conditions can vary, but performing at much lower temperatures than pyrometallurgy leads to lower energy costs for the recovery of lithium, graphite, manganese and aluminum. Nonetheless, hydrometallurgy is a long and difficult process for the recovery of metals because of the complexity of the composition of the leachate solution. Finally, this method can require a high consumption of chemical reagents and materials.

Pyrometallurgy and hydrometallurgy are processes that can also be combined to improve individual yields, but the drawbacks of each method remain problematic. A promising greener alternative, biometallurgy, is being studied nowadays at a laboratory scale but the process, based on the promotion of metal leaching by microbial activity, shows some non negligeable drawbacks [18].

Recycling techniques can be based on the adsorption process of metallic ions with organic or inorganic materials. Hydrometallurgy usually requires this technique among all the other separation methods. Here, the adsorption of a metal relies on the transfer of this particular metal from a fluid (liquid or gas) to a solid. The recovery of lithium with inorganic materials is reported for a large range of materials. For instance, aluminosilicates, activated carbons [26], aluminum oxides, aluminum hydroxides, iron phosphates, clay minerals, zeolite group minerals, zirconium phosphates, tin antimonates, antimony oxides, tantalum oxides, and niobium oxides have been considered [27]. However, they have not been sufficiently studied and reported sorption capacities are often low. The most developed and optimized systems today are Mn or Ti-based materials. They are generally used in ion exchange sieves that have the advantage of providing high capacity, selectivity and good recycling properties. A recent and very comprehensive review of inorganic materials for the recovery of lithium is available [28]. Mn and Ti-based materials may present great Li+ uptake and selectivity, but their synthesis (energy-consuming and high-temperature) and stability (degradation in low pH regeneration conditions) have not yet been fully optimized to be implemented in the recycling industry, leaving some room for improvement in the future.

The need for other versatile and efficient lithium-selective complexing groups has oriented the reported work towards the sorption with organic-based materials (crown ethers, aza-crown ethers or others organic agents). Organic compounds usually bear polar functional groups such as carboxyl, amines, hydroxyls, ethers or phosphates. These complexing groups can bind with metals according to various mechanisms such as ion exchange, covalent bonding, or chelation [29]. With such a variety of organic functional groups, organic complexing agents offer more flexibility and less consuming synthesises and processes compared to inorganic compounds, while maintaining the possibility to turn towards biobased materials.

Several reviews dealing with the recycling of lithium are available in the literature. These reviews focus essentially on general methods of recovering lithium from spent batteries or from natural resources (lakes, brines, seawater, etc.) by means of pyrometallurgy, hydrometallurgy with inorganic sorbents, or combinations of both [28,30]. As explained earlier, hydrometallurgy processes are more attractive compared to pyrometallurgy, because of their milder conditions. Reviews already reported in the literature mainly deal with complexing materials, or recycling processes in hydrometallurgy without specific complexing functions. The novelty of our approach deals with the challenges when combining both recycling processes and organic complexing materials. The overall procedure, from Lithium-ion battery leaching to the recovery of Lithium and the reuse of the extractant, was considered. In particular, we covered the following topics: i) efficiency of lithium-ion battery leaching processing, ii) production of a leachate and potential challenges related to its composition for lithium separation, iii) organic functions for selective complexation of lithium, iv) implementation of complexation agents into recycling processes, and v) desorption of lithium and reusability of the extractants.

The most relevant organic complexing materials for recycling lithium from wasted lithium-ion battery leachates in hydrometallurgy processes were identified. Nevertheless, as the literature is scarce on these types of effluents, while plenty of examples relate to the extraction of lithium from brines or seawater, we will also rely on other effluents to identify the most relevant complexing groups and the complexation mechanisms induced for lithium. First, a brief review of the repurposing of spent lithium-ion batteries and leaching conditions in the hydrometallurgy pathway will be discussed. Then, classic organic lithium-selective complexing agents such as crown ethers and aza-crown ethers will be described, followed by more unconventional and complex systems. In the third section, different ways of processing these complexing agents are discussed, whether they can be used in membrane designs, grafted onto solid supports or onto soluble polymers. In the fourth and final section, the recyclability and ageing of such process designs will be considered.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lithium-ion battery leaching processes and resulting leachate compositions
ce:section.ce:para: Before reviewing organic complexing agents and complexing processes, it is necessary to well characterize the initial lithium-ion batteries leachate solutions since parameters such as the initial concentration of metal ions in the LIB, the molar ratio between lithium and other metals, pH, ionic strength, etc. will have a great influence on the sorption capacity of the sorbents. The leaching of the cathode of a battery consists in exposing the material to an acidic solution in reducing conditions [31]. This part first presents the pre-treatment steps prior to leaching, then the leaching process is described with a special focus on the typical leachate solutions obtained during spent LIB recycling and the potential competing ions towards Li.

During the pre-treatment steps prior to hydrometallurgy, leaching can be used with optimum conditions so as to maximize the leaching efficiency and recover a maximum amount of metal ions in the leachate. Generally, optimum leaching conditions and recovery percentage per metal are reported. To give an idea of leaching conditions and efficiency (percentage of recovered metal compared to initial amount), Table 1
compares the composition of leachates resulting from different studies in the literature. Typically, the wasted LIB batteries that are leached originate from collected NMC, NCA or LFP batteries and may be mixed with Nickel-metal hydride batteries (NiMHs) [32]. Two main types of acidic solutions are reported to leach wasted batteries: inorganic acids and organic acids. Among the inorganic acids, sulfuric acid, hydrochloric acid, nitric acid and phosphoric acid are commonly used, while organic acids are molecules containing one or more carboxylic groups such as citric acid, ascorbic acid, maleic acid, lactic acid, etc. Both inorganic and organic acids are often used in combination with a reducing agent (very frequently hydrogen peroxide (H2O2)) to assist the dissolution of metal oxides from the cathodic material. Generally, the higher the concentration of H2O2, the more efficient the leaching is [15].

According to Table 1 , the leaching of metals, and particularly lithium, is quite efficient as it is generally above 90 %, whether the metals are leached with organic acids or inorganic acids. The advantage of using organic acids is that they have a higher pKa (above 0) and they may be bio-sourced (like citric acid for example), whereas inorganic acids may be more polluting to produce, and more difficult to manipulate due to their strong reactivity with air or water (pKa below 0). Another important parameter is the pulp density also known as the ratio between solids (cathodic material powder) and liquids (acid and reducing agent solutions). Finally, the rest of the operating conditions are all quite similar from one study to another: the leaching process is generally implemented at temperatures between 60 and 90 °C, with average durations and speeds of respectively 2 h and 300 rpm.

Although very few studies provide the pH of the leachate solutions, it seems to remain below 2.5, providing relatively acidic solutions. With regards to the leachate composition, lithium has a median concentration of 1.18 g/L. In addition to the lithium, leachates are mainly composed of base metals: cobalt, nickel, manganese, iron, aluminum, copper with respective median concentrations of 7.79, 1.68, 2.04, 0.24, 1.80 and 2.54 g/L. However, metals such as iron, copper and aluminum can easily be separated by adjustment of the pH to 6.5 and filtration of their hydroxides [33]. For this reason, depending on the step the leachate composition is evaluated, some metals may have already been removed.

According to our calculations, lithium is the second most abundant metal in number in the leachates yet one of the least abundant in mass due to its small atomic mass. There is an average of six times more ions of sodium than lithium (this result is questionable since only two articles mention the presence of sodium) while the weight of cobalt is almost seven times higher than that of lithium (almost the same mole percentage). Overall, a potential ionic competition of lithium with cobalt and/or sodium can be expected during the selective complexation of lithium. Other reasons for which ions can compete with lithium during complexation can be put forward, for instance because of a similar ionic radius (Lithium: 76 Å, Nickel: 69 Å, Cobalt: 74.5 Å) or identical electrical charges (Lithium and Sodium ions have one positive charge). Finally, it is noteworthy to highlight the data of Hamza et al. whose results seem to set the minimum for each ion in the leachate, perhaps due to the difference in LIB pre-treatment and the presence of remaining components such as plastics or electrolyte [41]. Similarly, some of the values in the study of Ningtyas et al. seem to set the maximum, yet we have not found the reason for such outstanding results [47].

To summarize this first section of the study, we suggest that a typical leachate solution is mostly composed of lithium, cobalt, nickel, manganese, iron, aluminum and copper with median concentrations going from 0.24 to 7.79 g/L and a pH below 2.5. While Fe, Al and Cu can easily be separated from the rest by forming hydroxides at higher pH, the main challenge is the separation of the remaining Li, Co, Ni and Mn. This information should help for the comprehension of the next parts of this review where the objective is to identify the main organic complexing agents that are selective towards lithium and how they can be implemented into recycling processes. For such applications, the competition with abundant cobalt or the possible presence of sodium must be kept in mind, as well as the acidic conditions, which can influence the efficiency and selectivity of the complexing systems towards lithium.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Organic complexation functions for the selective recovery of lithium
ce:section.ce:para: This part describes the main organic complexation functions for the selective recovery of lithium with a special focus on crown ethers (CEs) and aza-crown ethers, which are the most abundantly described. The complexation mechanisms, consisting in hydrogen bonding, physisorption, chemisorption or ion exchange, and their dependence on leachate characteristics are also discussed here. These complexation agents can be implemented as functional groups of soluble materials in a solvent (aqueous or extraction solution) or grafted on a solid support, such as a polymer or copolymer (polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) [58], polydopamine [59], polyglycidyl methacrylate (PGMA) [60], polyurethane [61], etc.), or on an inorganic surface to form hybrid systems (e.g. a silica support [62,63]). These materials can then be designed in different forms, as particles, beads, fibers, foams, membranes or even more complex geometries as discussed in Part IV.

Crown ethers are cyclic compounds containing several ether groups (-O-CH2-CH2-), known for the selective complexation of cations but also neutral and ionic organic species. The size of the crown defines the cation selectivity; this concept is also known as host-guest complexation. With an increasing size of the crown comes the possibility for complexing larger compounds. Fig. 2
compares the size of the most common crown ethers and the size of the corresponding cation they usually are selective of [64]. Crown ethers are noted as the number of atoms in the cycle, followed by the letter C (as in “crown”) and the number of oxygen atoms found in the crown. For instance, 9-crown-3 is a nine-atom long cycle containing three oxygen atoms.

Crown ethers have the advantage of being rather adaptable as they are soluble in both aqueous and lipophilic solvents [64]. They also allow for several sites where other functional groups can be grafted onto, to modify physico-chemical properties. In particular, the prefix “B” for benzo or “DB” for dibenzo are added in front of the CE's name in the case one or two benzo groups are attached to the CEs. In terms of complexation, CEs are the most abundantly described complexing functions for the selective recovery of lithium. Literature describing the effective and selective complexation of lithium with CEs is summarized in Table 3
. The sorption properties with respect to Li or other metal ions, which may be found in LIB leachates are then discussed by type of CEs groups.

First, it is important to note that the research described in the literature (Table 3 ) tends to be intended for the recovery of lithium from natural sources (seawater, Salt Lake brines…), and only a few studies aim at the recovery of lithium from LIBs. The behaviour of the CEs towards lithium in brine solutions may not be fully representative of the same behaviour in LIB leachate solutions, but it can give indications on the influence of some parameters on the global behaviour of CEs in general.

9-crown-3 provides the smallest cavity size among all crown ethers. To our knowledge, only De et al. report the study of the cavity size of 9C3s: a theoretical value of 0.2649 Å was found [93]. The lithium ion has a diameter of 1.56 Å, so the cavity size seems to be too small to host a lithium cation. However, according to the literature, 9C3s are known to be excellent complexing groups for beryllium (0.62 Å diameter), which is smaller than lithium, although still too big for the cavity size observed by De et al. [94–99]. Nevertheless, Oral et al. recently put great effort into synthesizing a lithium selective diblock copolymer functionalized with 9C3s [58]. The crown derivative is functionalized onto a polyacrylate organic material. In this work, the uptake of sodium and potassium is evaluated and proven to be insignificant compared to the lithium-ion uptake that reached 39 % of the lithium initially present. The explanation proposed by the authors is based on the stable 2:1 (mol:mol) sandwich complexes that are formed: the ion is not inside the cavity, but rather interacting in between two parallel planes of the CEs (Fig. 3
).

Previous reports confirm that the stability of sandwich formation is in fact higher as the ion-dipole interactions are doubled. The sandwich formation is enthalpically favorable as six oxygen atoms are interacting with the ion [100–102]. Additionally, there may be an increased stability due to easily ionizable unreacted methacrylates that can assist the complexation between lithium and CE-based polymer. Perhaps this extra function makes a difference for the sandwich complexation of lithium with a brush type polymer system.

As the size of the crown ether cavity increases, a selective complexation of lithium can occur. 12-crown-4 (12C4) and 14-crown-4 (14C4) are the most commonly reported complexing agents for the selective complexation of lithium (Fig. 4
.a). The cavity size of 12C4 is large enough to host a lithium cation on a 1:1 ratio, and is yet too small to host larger cations. Several examples are available in Table 3 . Overall, systems containing 12C4 ethers yield maximum Li+ uptakes ranging from 14.6 μg/gresin [63] to 27.5 mg/gpolymer [59] with generally good selectivity towards lithium over other ions (Na, K, Mg, Ca, etc.). Several noteworthy X-ray crystallography and thermodynamic studies have shown that 14C4, form six membered chelate rings with the complexed metal and provide more stable complexes than 12C4 systems (that form five membered chelate rings) [103]. The angle between a heteroatom, the metal cation that is complexed and the successive heteroatom is smaller in a five membered chelate ring than in a six membered one (Fig. 4 .b). According to their thermodynamic study, Hancock et al. showed that the wider the angle, the more stable the complexation. Therefore, it seems that using a 14C4 system provides improved complexation stability compared to 12C4 complexation systems. In other words, 14C4 should provide a better ionic adsorption capacity. However, this may lead to more difficult desorption cycles as the lithium has a better affinity with the adsorption system. This aspect will be discussed in Part V.

Examples in favor of Hancock's point of view are available in the literature. For instance, Bartsch et al. describe the selective complexation of lithium with 14C4-based molecules, i.e., that are not grafted into a macromolecular system [89]. With optimum conditions, the authors reached an 85 % extraction efficiency corresponding to approximately 59 mg of complexed lithium per gram of CE-based molecule (14C4 compound). This is fifteen times more than the system described by Luo et al. [67]. Regarding the selectivity factor, the system was 20 times more selective towards lithium than sodium and the other ions were not complexed.

More generally, the maximum Li+ uptake of 14C4-containing systems ranges from 3.1 to 444 mg/g of (macro)molecule (/g of compound if molecular, /g of polymer if the 14C4 is on a polymer support), which is globally better than 12C4 systems. However, this may also be attributed to the presence of carboxylic acid arms or pendant hydroxylic groups to compensate the wider size of the crown. This is the case for the examples described above [83,89], but also in other studies [82,86,87,90]. For instance, with regard to the Torrejos' system, the same molecule deprived of its carboxylic acid arm complexes lithium very poorly (5 % of Li+) compared to >85 % with a carboxylic acid function. DFT calculations show that Li+ can either be complexed i) in the CE (Fig. 5
.a), ii) with the pendant carboxylic group (Fig. 5 .b) or iii) in combination of both. Consequently, the side arms allow for increased complexation sites as well as slightly more favorable complex configurations as they have a lower binding energy [89].

Regarding the selectivity towards lithium, despite the larger cavity of 14C4s compared to 12C4s, the selectivity of such systems towards lithium seems to remain interesting, since the majority of the selectivity factors (Table 3 ) is rather high. So, the slight increase in CE size does not seem to drastically influence the selectivity towards lithium yet. Nevertheless, in some cases, the presence of sidearms may be a reason for the non-exclusive recovery of lithium. Contrary to crown ethers, OH and COOH groups are non-selective complexation sites. As their presence may improve complexation stability, they may also improve the complexation stability of other cations that have a strong affinity for hard bases such as carboxylate oxygens [104]. This matter may also be accompanied by physical retention of other metal ions on the surface of the adsorbent or possibly the complexing of larger metal ions that may occur if the spatial disposition allows for complexation agents to form 2:1 sandwich type coordination. These are some of the explanations proposed by Torrejos et al. for the non-negligible uptake of sodium in their multiwalled carbon nanotubes-supported dibenzo 14C4 ether with carboxyl sidearms [90]. This may also explain the poor selectivity in the system of Hankins et al. [63]. Finally, regarding the presence of benzo groups, 12C4s and 14C4s are used either without them, with one, or up to two benzo groups symmetrically attached to the CEs. The data retrieved from Table 3 shows that the sorption capacities of 12C4s and 14C4s can vary from 0.01 to 27 mg/g material and 3.1 mg/g (only one example retrieved) respectively, while in the presence of benzo groups, those values range from 0.21 to 8 mg/g and from 3.5 to 444 mg/g respectively. Although the sorption properties of CEs can depend on several other parameters, and more studies would be necessary especially for 14C4s, these values seem to indicate that the presence of benzo groups may cause lower sorption properties for 12C4s, while they may improve those of 14C4s. This observation was made in the work of Luo et al., where the Li and Na association constants with similar derivatives of a DB14C4, a B12C4 and a 12C4 were determined [88]. The results showed that the interactions of lithium with the B12C4 were much weaker than with its benzo-deficient equivalent. This was attributed to the rigidity of the benzo group in the context of Li sorption with 12C4. Meanwhile, the DB14C4 presented the best lithium sorption selectivity, as the wider structure of the CE provided more flexibility and availability.

Similarly to the 12C4s described above, aza-crown ethers are macrocyclic compounds containing ether groups, where at least one oxygen atom is replaced by a nitrogen atom. Very efficient systems have been developed with aza-crown ethers for the selective recovery of lithium as they have great advantages to stabilize the complexation process. The nitrogen groups are commonly exploited as they provide a simple opportunity of grafting side arms onto the CE, allowing a stabilizing effect during the complexation. Katayama et al. and Tsukube et al. both report the synthesis of aza-crown ethers bearing respectively pendant hydroxyls [78] and amine groups [80]. They prove that nitrogen chelating functional groups with pendant sidearms are excellent candidates for the selective extraction of lithium as they obtain good extraction results with such systems. Katayama's system only extracted lithium (over Na, K, Rb, Cs) with an extraction constant (−log Kex) of 9.7 while the 12C4 equivalent did not complex the lithium at all. Another example worth mentioning is the photochromic crowned spirobenzopyran as a highly selective extractant for lithium developed by Li et al. [79]. Li’s technology relies on the reversible covalent ether bond of the spiropyran, that can switch from a colorless ‘closed’ spiropyran under visible light to a colored ‘open' merocyanine under UV irradiation (Fig. 6
). The pendant anion of the open form allows for high complexation stability since the sorption capacity reaches 1.4 mol Li/mol, i.e., 19 mg Li/g complexing agent. As for the selectivity, Li et al. studied the influence of the concentration of Mg2+ and noted that the sorption capacity of lithium was barely impacted, even for an initial Mg/Li ratio of 1000:1 and [Li+] = 1 mM.

Literature dealing with the selective complexation of lithium with 15C5 is much less abundant, as the “size fit” concept is less and less applicable with the increasing cavity sizes. While 12C4 and 14C4 are more abundant in the literature given their cavity size selectivity, Sun et al. have synthesized larger benzo-15C5 (Fig. 7
), for the selective recovery of lithium from LIB leachates [92].

With optimal conditions, they obtained a maximum extraction efficiency of Li+ of 37 % compared to 5.18 % for Ni+ and 0 % for Mn2+ and Co2+. This study is of great interest as the conditions are the closest to LIB leachate conditions we have found, showing that crown ethers can clearly have selectivity towards lithium in the presence of battery leachate competitive ions. However, in this study, a 37 % extraction rate corresponds to a sorption capacity of roughly 1 mg/g of CE, which is rather scant compared to previously described examples. Additionally, lithium-selectivity is not systematic with 15C5 ethers: for instance, Seyedi et al. studied the selectivity of 15C5 derivatives in mixtures of Li, Na and K and the CEs selectively preferentially chelated the sodium and potassium ions, which indicated that the larger size of the cavity could host larger ions than lithium [105]. It is worth noting that some of the CEs above-mentioned (15C5 and smaller) are bound to one or several benzo group. Buschmann et al. attribute the use of benzo groups to a beneficial increase in stability as they reduce the basicity of the adjacent ether donor atoms and as a result the values of the reaction enthalpy decrease [106]. The DFT study by Yao et al. on Li+/15C5 and Li+/B15C5 shows that, in fact, the binding energy of B15C5 is smaller than that of 15C5, but the negative free energy is lower, which indicates that Li+ will more preferably complex with B15C5 [107]. Nevertheless, Buschmann also mentions that these same benzo groups reduce the flexibility of the ligands. This trend is noticeable for 15C5 and wider CEs [106].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Complexation mechanism for Li sorption on crown ethers
ce:section.ce:para:
Research regarding the complexation mechanisms of crown ethers with lithium and the nature of their bond during the complexation can be found in the literature. The attraction between cations and CEs or aza-CEs is of the ion-dipole type (electrostatic attraction) [108]. Shehata et al. have compared five different kinetic models to investigate the most applicable mechanism model for the complexation of radionuclides on 4,4′(5′)di-t-butylbenzo 18-crown-6 (DtBB18C6) grafted onto insoluble polymers [109]. When in heterogeneous phase (complexing group in solid phase / ions in aqueous phase), they established, through the applicability of a pseudo-second-order kinetic model, that the complexation is controlled by a particle diffusion mechanism, where the equilibrium is reached after two to three hours. Their thermodynamic study, in accordance with other studies [110,111], showed a chemisorption process of an endothermic nature. Nevertheless, the bonds between Li+ ion and crown ethers are more than likely stable coordination bonds, as shown in the literature [112,113]. The X-Ray crystal structure studies of lithium bis(trimethylsilyl)amide·12-crown-4 show that lithium is set in a coordination environment, where Li binds with all the oxygen atoms in the crown. Theoretical binding energies between Li+ ion and 12C4, 15C5 and 18C6 CEs are abundantly described in the literature. Some of these results are summarized in Table 4
. In each study, the binding of Li with 15C5 has the tendency of having the lowest energy compared to other CEs, indicating that Li+ can complex more preferably with 15C5. This is due to the small and adapted size of the complex compared to lithium, as well as the fact that it offers more available oxygen groups for complexation stability (five instead of four in 12C4). As a consequence, Li/15C5 complexes exhibit more stable bonds than Li/12C4 complexes, however it is important to keep in mind that the selectivity of the wider CE may shift towards larger ions than lithium as mentioned earlier. Additionally, with the increasing size of the CE, the synthesis and purification may become more difficult.

Finally, the nature of the complexation mechanisms as well as the lithium uptake and selectivity may be influenced by several parameters defined by the physico-chemical properties of the ion-containing solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solvent water content and influence on the hydration of lithium
ce:section.ce:para: Because cations have different intrinsic hydration enthalpies, their capability of interacting with organic complexing agents strongly depends on the water content of the solvent. Hence, there is a great difference whether the complexation is studied in aqueous media (in the majority of cases), or organic solvents with a high or very low water content. More particularly, lithium and sodium, which are close in size, are both good candidates for the selective complexation by 12C4s. They have a solvation enthalpy of respectively −514 and − 406 kJ/mol in water [117]. Lithium is slightly smaller in size, allowing a larger number of water molecules around it and a bigger solvation enthalpy, compared to sodium, which is slightly bigger in size and can interact with less water molecules hence a smaller solvation enthalpy. This indicates that, depending on the nature of the solvent (aqueous or organic with a certain water content), lithium may be more difficult to complex with CEs, as it has a bigger affinity with water than sodium ions do. Additionally, sodium ions may form perching complexes (ion is above the crown) which still allows for a sufficient interaction to compete with nesting complexation of lithium (ion is inside the crown) in 15C5s for example [118].

Studies describing the interfering of competing ions such as sodium or cobalt are available in the literature [63,92]. Notably, Warnock et al. described a filtration process using a polynorbornene-based membrane platform with 12C4 functions. The membrane presented unusual “reverse permeability selectivity for LiCl over NaCl” in water, meaning that 12C4 inside the membrane complexed the sodium ions while they were permeable to lithium (lithium passed through the membrane), i.e., preferred selectivity towards sodium [70]. Simulations showed that Na+ was more easily dehydrated compared to Li+, so the authors observed that NaCl salts had a better solubility (i.e., less coordinated water molecules) and lower diffusivity relative to LiCl leading to a better affinity of Na+ with 12C4.

Studies of the influence of counterions on the complexation of lithium with crown ethers are scarce and were mostly conducted before 2000, with little to no recent research available to our knowledge. Initially, counterions were known to have a negative effect on the complexation of metallic cations onto CEs as the negative charge on the counterion presents a certain competition with the electronegativity of the CEs towards cations. According to De Jong, this negative influence of counterions was often simply minimized either by using high dilution conditions or by choosing anions whose salts are better dissociated in solution such as tetraphenylborate [119]. Other studies have been performed since then, with notably the work of Danil de Namor et al. who compared the effect of a few anions on the coordination process between lithium and crown ethers. For instance, oxyanions such as perchlorate anion can interact with the metal cation through the oxygen atoms in several ways, enabling some coordinating ability and stabilizing effects [120]. More generally, Lissa Nuri et al. explained that the extraction of lithium by a CE is affected by the solvation energy of the accompanying anion, i.e., the anion-solvent interaction [121]. They studied the effect of the counterion on the extraction of lithium with 18C6 ethers and showed that extractability of 18C6 decreased following this order I− > NO3 − > ClO3 − > Cl− and was almost null with the sulfate ion. This was in accordance with the Hofmeister or lyotropic series of anions [122], that differentiate two types of counterions: i) the kosmotropic anions, that are strongly hydrated and tend to keep their counter-cation more tightly associated with water (Co3 2−, SO4 2−, etc.); ii) the chaotropic anions, which are on the other end of the Hofmeister series and tend to be less strongly hydrated, allowing the cation to complex with an extractant (ClO4 −, I−, NO3 −, etc.).

During the leaching process of LIBs, the cathode material dissociates and turns into metallic salts. The counterions depend on the acid and reducing agent that are initially used for the leaching: SO4 2−, Cl−, NO4 2−, PO4 2− and carboxylate forms of organic acids can be found. Consequently, using appropriate leaching agents with chaotropic counterions (nitric acid for instance) would be of best interest to improve the following extraction steps of the metals.

Several studies have been conducted to better understand the influence of pH on the complexation capacity of crown ethers. The general trend is to work with an optimal pH of at least 6, as sorption capacity of lithium usually increases significantly to reach a maximum for pH = 6 and then slightly decreases for a higher pH. This observation is recurrent in several studies in the literature [62,65,67,71–73,75,92,123] and is due to the fact that at a lower pH, the free hydrogen protons can associate with the donor oxygen groups in CEs. This constitutes a complexation competition with lithium ions and negatively influences the complexation capacity of CEs for lithium, whereas at higher pH, the protons are much less available for this type of competition [75]. In certain cases, a more drastically basic pH is clearly preferable to enable enhanced lithium complexation. For instance, this is a general observation for CEs bearing side arms. This is well illustrated in the work of Torrejos et al., as the carboxylic acid side arm present in the form of carboxylate ion at pH > 7 can serve as a stabilizing counter-ion for the coordinated Li+ [90]. Nevertheless, the pH of a solution may have a great influence on the speciation of the metallic ions in this solution. While lithium easily dissociates no matter the parameters, the speciation of other ions such as nickel, cobalt or manganese remains much more complex. The speciation of an ion is also dependant on the concentration and the counterion. As a consequence, the pH, the concentration and the counterion must be chosen adequately so that the lithium ions are in their dissociated form and available to complexation.

There are only few studies regarding the influence of the temperature on the complexation of lithium with CEs. Generally, what is reported is that this parameter has no or little influence on the complexation of lithium [75,123], so experiments are usually performed at room temperature. However, Sun et al. have shown that the extraction rate of lithium ions with CEs can be improved from approximately 20 % to 30–35 % by increasing the temperature from 15 °C to 30 °C [92]. The authors explain that the combination of metal ions and CEs is exothermic, which should cause improved sorption properties at low temperatures. But the opposite was observed in this study: the sorption properties were better at higher temperatures. The endothermic processes such as the desolvation of metal ions and the structural rearrangement of the complex of metal ions and CEs prevailed over the exothermic character of the metal/Ce interaction. The intensity of these endothermic processes depends on the ion: for instance, it is more remarkable for lithium ions than cobalt ions in Sun's study. In other words, this implies that, at higher temperatures, the complexation of lithium is thermodynamically favored over the complexation of cobalt, inducing a better separation coefficient than at lower temperatures. Similarly, Torrejos et al. studied the effect of temperature on the complexation of lithium, sodium and potassium with CEs [89]. They noted that an increase in temperature from 25 to 50 °C induced a slight increase in lithium extraction, while sodium and potassium were extracted up to three times more. The higher sensitivity of sodium and potassium extractions with temperature was attributed to the use of ionic liquids in the extraction process, and for future references, the authors performed studies at room temperature to maintain the best extraction selectivity towards lithium.

Crown ethers provide optimal conditions for the selective recovery, extraction or separation of lithium, as they respond to a simple host-guest complexation mechanism. More particularly, 12C4s and 14C4s are most abundantly described as lithium, the smallest metal, can fit inside their cavity and interact with the heteroatoms along the cycle, while 9C3s have a cavity that is too small and 15C5s are slightly too large to ensure selectivity every time. The 12C4 and 14C4 ethers provide compelling maximum lithium uptakes as well as a certain selectivity over many other ions, such as alkali metals (sodium, potassium, rubidium or cesium) or alkaline earth metals (magnesium, calcium). Their sorption properties can be improved by modifying their environment to help the stabilization of the chelation mechanism, by using benzo groups or carboxylic sidearms for instance. However, these performances must be considered and compared carefully, as they can easily vary depending on the experimental conditions, but also the structure of the support and the way they are implemented (See Part IV). Because of this, the sorption properties are difficult to compare as sorption capacities are expressed in various units, making it more difficult to understand the mechanistic behaviours. Another important remark, which is mentioned earlier, is that most of the articles report the recovery of lithium from natural sources (seawater, Salt Lake brines, etc.), and rarely from LIBs. This still gives a great indication on how lithium behaves in the presence of CEs, and the promising work of Nisola et al. can only show the potential of such easily synthesized functions in the processing of recovering lithium from wasted LIB leachates [60]. In the end, the 12C4 ethers are the most interesting CEs according to the literature, as they present the most adapted size compared to lithium and the literature is abundant in this sense. Consequently, the sorption behaviour of 12C4s in LIB leachate solutions would be worthy to investigate, as it has never been reported.

Other types of organic complexation agent that are selective towards lithium are summarized in Table 5 and include systems that are similar to crown ethers, such as ketone-containing macrocyclic compounds, cryptands or calixarenes, but also open/linear versions like oligo ethylene glycols. Other examples include heteroatom-rich and/or deprotonable molecules like phosphor- or cyclitol-based extractants.

While macrocyclic compounds may present advantageous properties, it is important to note that their synthesis and purification can be challenging, as other cyclic or linear by-products can be obtained. Additionally, they are all used in their molecular state (with no polymer support), and the solubility of such compounds is rather limited to mostly some organic solvents. For this reason, they are used in liquid-liquid extractions, a process that also has its drawbacks (See Part IV.3).

Cryptands are bicyclic or tricyclic (-O-CH2-CH2-) ether compounds generally containing nitrogen atoms at the bridgehead positions (Table 5 ). They were also studied in the literature for their selectivity towards lithium ions. For instance, Lehn et al. were the first to synthesize a cryptand C211 (each figure describing the number of oxygens in each bridge of the molecule) [131]. This cryptand showed selective complexation of lithium with a stability constant (log Ks) of 5.5 for lithium against 3.2 for Na+, and <2 for other ions. Interestingly, if the cryptand is widened by just one unit (cryptand C221), the molecule becomes no longer selective to lithium but to sodium ion. The authors explain that it is once again due to the size-fit concept, which is even more applicable here as the additional bridge increases the rigidity of the complexation agent, compared to simple CEs. Shamsipur et al. studied the influence of several parameters on the exchange mechanisms of lithium ion with cryptand C221 [145]. 7Li NMR studies had been previously conducted on cryptand C211 and revealed that no matter the solvent, two signals were observed [146]. They corresponded to free Li+ ion and complexed Li+ ion, suggesting the simultaneous presence of free and complexed Li+ during the complexation experiments. Furthermore, Shamsipur et al. conducted similar studies with cryptands C221 and C222 and found only one signal, indicating a fast exchange with the cryptands and inexistant free Li+ ions. A study on the effect of temperature on the mechanisms of cryptands C221 and C222 in methanol was conducted. Results showed that at low temperatures (below −30 °C), a bimolecular mechanism dominated, while at temperatures above 5 °C, the dissociative pathway prevailed. The prevailing bimolecular mechanism at low temperature was more than likely due to a more important enthalpic contribution [145]. A few years later, the same observation was made by Shirai et al. [147].

Similarly to the macrocyclic compounds, cryptands are large compounds that are rather difficult to implement into a process. Consequently, research regarding the sorption of lithium with such molecules is rather out-dated and unreported since.

More complex structures such as calixarenes are also available and studied for the recovery of lithium. They consist in cup-shaped molecules (Table 5 ), with hydroxyl functions at their base, allowing functionalization or chemical bonding onto a surface for example. Wang et al. recently developed calix[4]arenes functionalized dual-imprinted mesoporous films for the simultaneous and selective recovery of lithium and rubidium [132]. Focusing on the part that is destined to Li+ ions, the system presented a maximum sorption capacity of 16 mg/g of material, and selectivity factors towards lithium largely superior to 70 for each ion. Ludwig et al. wrote a comprehensive review on calixarene-based molecules for cation recognition [148]. They notably detailed lithium-ion recognition of calix[4]arenes that are the most suited for Li+ complexation. Due to the nature of its wide hydration shell, lithium is not complexed inside the cavity of the calixarene, but on its rim. Selectivity towards lithium over sodium can be controlled and improved by equipping the calixarene with nitrophenol or azophenol moieties, for instance. Nevertheless, calixarenes are rather onerous and more difficult to implement as they are voluminous molecules [149].

One method to imitate crown ethers, while conveying greater flexibility, is to exploit oligo ethylene glycols, which can wrap themselves around ions, in the same manner as crown ethers, but with a much greater scope. This notion was first discovered in the mid-70s and known as octopus molecules notably with the work of Vögtle et al. [150]. The researchers synthesized hexasubstituted benzene derivative containing long sulfur supported polyethers, producing octopus shaped molecules (Fig. 8
) with a strong affinity towards alkali ions, but no selectivity towards lithium. In fact, the oligo ethylene glycols designed by Koo et al. were not selective towards lithium either, since optimal conditions enabled a maximum lithium uptake of only 38 % against 92 % for cobalt. In the end, the study did lead to a process that separated Li+ and Co2+ ions, which is described in part IV.

Cyclitols are cyclic alkane compounds bearing at least three hydroxyl groups on three different carbon atoms of its cycle (Fig. 9
). They are biobased compounds playing a particularly important role in cell functioning of plants. For more information about cyclitols and its many derivatives, an in-depth review on cyclitols is available in the literature [151].

Lithium-selective cyclitol-based compounds are reported in the literature. The infatuation for such a compound is due to the numerous reactive groups that cyclitol offer: the oxygen groups can be used to graft sidearms for the selective metal complexation, while other oxygens can be used for further functionalization to prepare polymeric or polymer-supported compounds. Sureshan et al. designed O-protected myo-inositol derivatives to form open chain analogues of crown-6-ethers hanging from two oxygen atoms with varying relative configurations [142]. Their best systems delivered excellent selectivity towards lithium with an association constant towards lithium that was at least 15 times higher than all the other ions. A similar study was conducted by the same team on cyclitol derivatives with veritable crown ethers grafted onto the oxygen atoms of its cycle [152]. However, the complexation of lithium was not favored, as the cyclized versions of previously discussed systems produces crown-6-ethers, that are too big to stably and selectively complex lithium. The main challenge with such molecules is to identify and understand the effect of the relative orientation of the added sidearms or CE on the efficiency and selectivity of complexation towards lithium. Dixit et al. studied this influence with 12C4 ethers grafted in a di-axial, di-equatorial or axial-equatorial position. In the di-axial configuration, the oxygen atoms attached to the inositol ring are separated by three carbon atoms, placing them closer to each other due to their di-axial position, leading to a higher separation factor and thus a better selectivity towards lithium [91,143].

Another example of molecular modifications is the functionalization and use of acid groups such as sulfonic acids to recover ionic species [144]. Hamza et al. reported the sulfonation of chitosan which provided a great amount of available sulfonic groups for the recovery of lithium [41]. The lithium uptake reached a high value of 138 mg/g of polymer. Additionally, the latter presented great selectivity towards lithium over Fe, Zn, Mg, Ca, Ni and Al. The selectivity could even be improved in the presence of oxalic acid, which complexed the competitive metals and increased the selectivity of the sulfonated chitosan material for lithium. The identified mechanisms with the various heteroatom-rich material (NH2, OH and SO3H) were ion-exchange (necessitating the protonated version of the acids, and thus an acidic pH equal to 2) and interactions associated with the tautomerization of the sulfone groups. As a consequence, the mechanisms were different from those observed with chelating cyclic compounds, and the selectivity of deprotonable groups could greatly shift, depending on parameters such as pH.

Carboxylic acids are another type of deprotonable groups which present advantageous properties, for instance with the use of polyacrylic acid (pAA) (one of the simplest polymers with a high density of carboxylic acid). As mentioned earlier, carboxylic acids are often exploited to improve the sorption properties of CEs. However, pAA is reported in the literature as a very performant sorbent of its own, for various metals, such as Cd, Pb, Ca, Cu, etc. as studies show that the polymer can reach retention rates higher than 90 % [153–155]. However, the studies of pAA for the separation of lithium from other metals is scarce, yet it has great potential and deserves to be further investigated [156–158].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion for the Li complexing groups
ce:section.ce:para: To conclude, many lithium-selective complexing functions are described in the literature but most of them have drawbacks that have not yet been resolved to allow industrial implementation to be considered. Macrocyclic compounds and calixarenes are voluminous functions, which may be onerous and difficult to upscale. If phosphor-based complexes show promising results (although the mechanisms of their selectivity towards lithium are still rather unclear to this day), they often require the use of ionic liquids and other assisting extractants to optimize the recovery, which can also be difficult to implement into a recycling process. CE-containing cyclitols, cryptands and oligoethylene glycols have structures that are the closest to CEs, yet the literature is quite scarce concerning their capability to selectively recover lithium. Meanwhile, crown ethers are the most described functional groups for the recovery of lithium in aqueous effluents because they follow the host guest complexation mechanism, where the size of the CE cavity defines the size of the ions it can chelate. In particular, 14C4s and especially 12C4s present the most interesting properties as their cavity is most adapted to lithium. They also have the advantage of being adaptable, as their environment can be easily modified with other beneficial functional groups such as aromatics or carboxylic acids that can add value in some cases and can be integrated into various processes, which is the purpose of the following part of this review. Concerning the use of carboxylic acids alone, or deprotonable functions more generally, it would be interesting to investigate their behaviour in the context of LIB recycling, as they are very attractive for their simplicity (in structure, and for their synthesis), allowing for the design of sorbents with high function density.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Implementation of complexation agents into recycling processes
ce:section.ce:para: In the literature, the selective separation of lithium from aqueous media involves a wide range of complexation functions described above. In order to use these lithium-selective groups to collect lithium from any kind of aqueous solution, they need to be implemented into a separation process. The most commonly used methods rely on the transfer of ions between different phases. A liquid-liquid transfer occurs when the complexation agent is present in an organic phase in contact with the aqueous phase containing lithium, whereas a liquid-solid transfer is involved in sorption processes where the complexation groups are grafted onto an organic or inorganic solid material which may be immobile (column, membrane) or free in suspension. Finally, a single-phase separation process can be considered, where the water-soluble and the ions are in the same phase. Each of these processes are described below, along with the challenges they may present.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mobile solid supports for the complexation of lithium
ce:section.ce:para: Support materials for the lithium-selective complexation functions may be non-soluble in water, in the form of macro, micro or nano-shaped particles. These materials are commonly used as mobile supports; i.e., the material is directly immersed into the aqueous solution containing the mix of ions to be removed. An extraction step is necessary to recover the particles from the suspension, this is regularly done by simple filtration (with no further details) or else, authors let the particles settle, by decantation, or centrifugation, and recover the supernatant to characterize the complexation capacity. Nevertheless, particles can also be used as an immobile support, under a dynamic flow process (for example in a column), as described in part IV.2. Here, the various possible designs of the materials are reported from a chemical point a view, and their implementation into a recycling process is discussed.

A wide range of neutral particle/bead systems has been reported in the literature for the selective recovery of lithium from various sources. Lithium-selective functions may be grafted onto inorganic mobile supports or onto polymeric chains or networks. A variety of examples are described in the literature and summarized in Table 6
.

Typically, neutral supports are functionalized with lithium-selective functions (frequently CEs). Neutral supports may be composed of inorganic networks (such as tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS)) [62,132], carbon [90], graphene oxides [68] or polymers. For instance, according to Torrejos et al., multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) have high specific surface area which is favorable for adsorption contact and functionalization of CEs [90]. Organic polymers bearing sorption agents are the most abundantly described. Alexandratos et al. published an in-depth review concerning the grafting of crown ethers onto polymeric systems and the resulting metal ion affinities [163]. Grafting crown ethers onto polymeric systems allows for better handling, recyclability and adaption to continuous processes. They can be grafted through step-growth polymerization, chain-growth polymerization, or through post-functionalization of pre-formed polymers. They are either linear (PGMA, PVDF, etc.), or crosslinked through radical polymerization (MAA with EDGMA, AA with MBAM, PGMA, GMA and DVB, etc.) or through polyaddition (CE-containing diol oligomer with di- and tri-phenylmethane diisocyanate). In particular, PGMA is an interesting candidate, due to its negligible affinity towards cations and high density of cleavable epoxide groups for CE functionalization [60]. Additionally, MMA is known for its stable chemical and mechanical properties, guaranteeing a stable and durable matrix for the adsorbents [71,73]. Particles with magnetic (Fe3O4) cores, a silica layer (to increase the high grafting ratio and stability of the system under acidic conditions) and a polymeric final layer are also reported [67,73]. Usually, the filtration of the particles is necessary in order to recover the adsorbent (bearing its Li+) from the aqueous solution containing other ions. To avoid the use of the filtration process (and the inevitable need to renew the filter after it being worn out), the strategy of extracting particles is based on an original way using iron oxides particles removed under a magnetic field. However, despite their easy processability, magnetic functionalized particles require difficult synthesis. Additionally, the use of acidic conditions to release the sorbed metals could degrade the magnetic particles after a certain exposure. There are several ways to improve diffusivity of ions towards the functional complexing functions. The majority of the described materials are spherical particles with diameters ranging from 50 nm [77] to 2 mm [160]. However, the choice of a fibrous/ tubular configuration of particles can allow promotion of Li+ ion diffusivity [60]. The fine diameter of fibers provides shorter diffusion path lengths for ions, better accessibility and the linear form of fibers allows for better permeation in between the fibers. For these reasons, fiber configuration could likely improve the adsorption kinetics of lithium and probability of contact between ions and adsorption groups, i.e., favoring faster adsorption rates. The use of porous materials also allows for enhanced diffusivity of ions towards the complexing sites. Pores are either formed during polymerization of materials, or imprinted with a removable hard or soft template (Nanocrystalline cellulose (NCC) or hexadecyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB)) [62,132]. According to Table 6 , pore volumes range from 0.006 cm3/g [71] to 0.3977 cm3/g [132] and sizes range from 2 nm [62] to 6 μm [160]. Typically, the surface area of non porous configurations ranges from 80 m2/g [68] to 98 m2/g [77] while porous particles have surface areas that can reach 177 m2/g [72], 300 m2/g [132] and even 1140 m2/g [62]. Finally, hydrophilic hydrogels are known for their great ability to swell in water, allowing for facilitated ion transport through the network, which can clearly improve the diffusivity of ions and enhance Li+ uptake onto CE [66,161,162].

It can be noted that all the studies reported (Table 6 ) have only focused on preliminary characterizations of the solid supported complexation functions, and no further pilot-scale processes have been reported. The maximum sorption capacities of such configurations range from 0.13 mg/gsupport [160] to 16 mg/gsupport [132]. It is of general knowledge that, for a given mass of a material, the more a material has pores and surface groups, the bigger the surface exchange is, which should allow for an improved sorption capacity of ions. For instance, the TEOS particles bearing calix[4]arene reported by Wang et al. have a high surface area of 299.79 m2/g and presented the highest Li+ uptake of 16 mg/gsupport [132]. Nevertheless, particles with the highest surface area (1143.56 m2/g) [62] provided an uptake of only 1.8 mg/gpolymer with very similar support compositions and experimental conditions as for Wang et al. This can be explained by a possible difference in complexing group density in the materials, where the particles with a high surface area may have fewer complexing groups than Wang's system for a same given surface area. Unfortunately, no information relative to the density of functional groups is given in neither of the studies. Another explanation for such a difference in lithium uptake can be related to a difference in preparation of the particles: Wang's system was prepared by ion-imprinting while the particles prepared by Xu et al. were not. For the latter, a recurrent and noteworthy technique is employed to obtain polymer-based particles or membranes. It consists of including, during the polymer synthesis or during the grafting of the complexing function, a template ion towards which the polymer is theorized to be selective. This ensures that there is a space, molded into the polymer, in which only a specific ion can exclusively fit. Once the synthesis is completed, the same ion is extracted (support is washed with an acidic solution), and the polymer is then ready for the extraction process.

Hydrogels are known for their excellent interactions with water, which should allow for better ion diffusivity, yet the reported sorption capacities of hydrogels varied from 0.21 mg/gpolymer [162] to 1.4 mg/gpolymer [66]. For instance, Park et al. designed phosphonate metal-organic framework (pMOF)/alginate composites [161,162]. Surprisingly, this complex hydrogel exhibited selectivity towards lithium ions. The unique environment of alginate-Al3+ coordination displayed strong repulsion force of cations by Al3+ ions. This strong repulsion hindered the approach of stronger electro-withdrawing ions such as Na+ or K+, while Li+ ions (with lower adsorption affinity) were more effectively adsorbed via physisorption. The fabricated composite had a selectivity factor of 3.06 for lithium over sodium, despite the high concentration and strong adsorption affinity of sodium ions. However, the maximum Li+ uptake in a lithium-containing solution was 0.033 mmol/gpolymer corresponding to 0.21 mg/g polymer, which is relatively low in comparison with to other reported systems.

Finally, ion exchange resins (IERs) were also studied for their selectivity towards lithium. IERs are porous beads composed of an insoluble crosslinked polymer backbone, and functional groups that are electrically active (ions). In the presence of a targeted ion, the ions carried by the ion exchange resin are permuted with the target ions in the solution. The reported IERs presented an excellent uptake of Li+ ions: 73.13 mg/g of resin for the system of Amalia et al. for instance [159]. Unfortunately, none of the studied IERs were fully selective towards lithium, which may be due to the lack of lithium-selective complexing functions as described in Part III. Only Hayashita et al. report the use of DB14C4-containing IERs for the recovery of lithium, but the CEs incorporated into the backbone of the polymer network did not selectively complex lithium [86]. Finally, Fukuda et al. report the lithium adsorption capacity of more than thirty commercial IERs in LiCl solutions [164]. The best IERs can reach an approximate sorption capacity of 30 mg/g of resin. However, when exposed to brine solutions with high salinity, the same resins presented very poor adsorption towards lithium due to the presence of high proportions of competing species.

In the end, solid-supported systems allow for a great deal of advantages. Combined with hydrogel compositions, or micro fibrous/porous properties, they confer increased diffusivity of ions and therefore enhance adsorption properties ranging from 0.13 to 138 mg/g for the best systems. No solvent is necessary, less sophisticated material is necessary for experimental procedures and the extraction procedure can thus be further simplified if the particles are magnetic. Additionally, microsphere polymerizations can be prepared under mild conditions which are more environmentally friendly and energy-efficient than inorganic ion-sieve adsorbents (which are synthesized under extremely high temperatures). Nevertheless, for the nanoparticles, several difficulties are observed for their application in large-scale processes: they have a tendency to agglomerate, they can leachate in the treated effluents, and their separation from aqueous solutions can be difficult [165]. Larger particles can be more easily separated (decantation, microfiltration), but to the best of our knowledge, no pilot-scale processes using mobile solid supports were reported for the recovery of lithium from aqueous solutions. Additionally, more attractive processes will be presented later in this review.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Immobile solid supports for the complexation of lithium
ce:section.ce:para: In part IV.1, the described solid supports were mobile in the aqueous solution, and a supplementary extraction step to recover the Li-containing solid supports was necessary. An easy and commonly used way to avoid this extraction step is to use a fixed solid support, and allow for the feed solution to flow through the lithium-selective material. This is typically a membrane process or a column setup.

Membranes are selective barriers between two different compartments. They can be used at room temperature, and have great adaptability for functionalization. Porous membranes separate compounds according to their size: compounds with a diameter size smaller than the size of the pores are separated from those with a diameter that is superior to the pore size. Porous membranes are applicable in the macroporous scale (50 nm < d < 500 nm = microfiltration) but also the mesoporous scale (2 < d < 50 nm = ultrafiltration) and the microporous scale (d < 2 nm = nanofiltration). Such membranes are used to recover nano, micro or macro particles from aqueous solutions, for instance the particles described in part IV.1. Membranes can also separate compounds according to the variation of chemical affinity between the compounds and the membrane composition [166,167]. These membranes are most commonly designed with solid polymers as polymers have the advantage of providing a wide range of structural and mechanical properties and a great adaptability. Furthermore, they can be easily functionalized with complexing agents for instance, allowing the chemical selectivity for membrane separation. Such functionalized membranes for the selective sorption of lithium are abundantly studied in the literature. It is interesting to note that several studies report preliminary batch adsorption studies (membranes are submerged into the aqueous solution and removed after a certain amount of time to dose the amount of Li+ left), whereas membranes are intended to be used in a dynamic system. The preliminary batch experiments are interesting to easily characterize the sorption capacity of the membrane, however they do not give any information on the permeability of the membrane.

In a first part, a brief description of the possible membrane designs and preliminary characterizations are given. Then, the implementation of such lithium-selective membranes into a pilot-scale process is described in depth.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Polymeric membrane designs and preliminary characterizations
ce:section.ce:para:
CE-containing membranes are reported in Table 7
, as well as their effectivity and selectivity towards the sorption of lithium. The choice of the polymer composition can be motivated by its price, its availability, its environmental impact, its easy handling, its final application or simply according to the field of research of a specific laboratory. PVDF and PDA are commonly used together since they present great physico-chemical properties and enable easy grafting of CEs post polymerization. Lithium selective complexing agents are in fact mostly grafted onto the already-made polymer membrane (sorption agent is a pendant group), however they may also be blended by incorporating it into a monomer with one reactive site (pendant group [168]) or more (sorption agent is part of the chemical structure of the polymer [70]).

Popular interest for biobased membranes has also emerged over the past few years. Cheng et al. designed chitosan-based membranes for the effective selective adsorption of lithium, by grafting epichlorohydrin-functionalized 12C4 onto available amine and hydroxyl groups of chitosan [169]. Chitosan is a linear polysaccharide fiber derived from chitin, a biopolymer that can be found in exterior shells of crabs, shrimp and lobsters. Previous studies have proven that chitosan alone could complex with metal ions because of its large number of available active groups (amine and hydroxyl) on its surface [170–172]. Functionalized with lithium-selective CEs, Cheng's design revealed to be exceptional as the chitosan-CE system reached an excellent maximum lithium adsorption capacity of 297 mg/gpolymer and good selectivity over Ca, K, Mg and Na. Such an uptake may also be due to the promising technology of nanofibrous membrane configurations [169,173]. The chitosan membranes were formulated into nanofibers inside the membrane with a fiber diameter of 40–80 nm.
