Similarly, Zheng et al. designed nanofibers containing chitosan, graphene oxide and 12C4s with a fiber diameter of 60–100 nm. These two systems exhibited the best lithium uptakes (respectively 297 and 142 mg/gpolymer) compared to the other PVDF based membranes reported (25.58 to 40 mg/gpolymer).

Finally, the ion-imprinted technique is also applied in the synthesis of Li+-selective membranes. The work of Sun et al. shows that ion-imprinted systems have a better maximum sorption capacity (27.1 mg/gpolymer) towards Li+ than non-ion-imprinted systems (19.2 mg/gpolymer) [69]. This highlights the purpose of the ion-imprinting technique, which is to create abundant micropores in the final material and provide a high-affinity recognition cavity of Li+ in the imprinted layers of the system. This trend is in alignment with plenty of other studies on ion-imprinted polymeric membranes for the sorption of lithium [57,59,65]. Naturally, classic non-imprinted membranes are also reported in the literature [85,169] and could potentially present better sorption capacities had they been synthesized with the ion-imprinted technique.

These preliminary results obtained in batch reactors are promising as they exhibit the best maximum uptakes overall, but are not fully adapted to characterize the membranes' capabilities to recover lithium in industrial processes. For a larger scale of extraction, membranes must be characterized by their permeability (which helps measure the amount of feeding solution that is treated) and the retention (which helps characterize the efficiency and selectivity towards lithium). This provides important indications on their practical usability in membrane sorption processes, and can help avoid issues such as slow permeation or clogging, as membranes may be prone to fouling.

In depth studies of pilot-level membrane filtration processes are summarized in Table 8
and Table 9
. Generally, two types of membrane adsorption processes are industrially used: dead-end membrane filtration and tangential (cross) flow filtration. In the first, the membrane is placed perpendicularly to the feed stream and the feed is forced through the membrane, whereas in the second, the feed flow is parallel to the membrane. To our knowledge, only one tangential configuration is reported for the recovery of lithium, yet tangential configurations retard clogging issues that are recurrent in dead-end configurations. Additionally, membrane filtrations can be performed by osmosis, using dense membranes, or under pressure, using porous membranes. In dynamic sorption processes, membranes are characterized by the following parameters: the permeation flux J (mg.cm−2.s−1), the permeability coefficient P(cm2.s−1) and the permselectivity factor β.

where V, A and d represent the volumes of feeding and receiving solutions (mL), the effective membrane area (cm2), and the membrane thickness, respectively. ΔCx/Δt represents the change of concentration in the receiving solution, and (CFc – CRx) is the concentration difference between feeding and receiving chambers.

Dense membranes are typically used in a typical U-model or H-model glass tube, which is initially composed of a chamber with the lithium-containing solution (A) and a second chamber of deionized water (B), separated by the polymer membrane (Fig. 10
). With dense membranes, an ideal filtration implies that the membrane exclusively complexes lithium and perfectly permeates other ions. The equilibrium would lead to the fixation of lithium onto the membrane, while i) the feeding solution may still contain lithium ions, and ii) the feeding and receiving solutions contain similar concentration of all the other ions. Unfortunately, the complexation functions reported earlier were proven to be selective but not exclusive towards lithium, leading to the possible retention of other ions onto the membrane, as well as the permeation of lithium ions into the receiving solution (i.e., loss of Li+ ions). The worst-case scenario was observed with the non-ion-imprinted membranes (NIIM), which presented poor selectivity towards lithium since the ratio of ions (including Li+) in the receiving solution was similar to the feeding solution [57,65,69]. However, ion-imprinted membranes were capable of allowing free penetration of all competitive ions while the permeation flux of Li+ was considerably slower. For instance, in the work of Lu et al., the permeation fluxes of Li+, Na+ and K+ were respectively 37.5 × 10−5, 273.17 × 10−5 and 362.5 × 10−5 mg/cm2/s, indicating a selectivity towards lithium over sodium of 7.39 and over potassium of 9.86.

Dense membrane filtrations give good indications on the capacities of membranes, but are not quite adapted to the industrial scale, due to slow kinetics and the fact that the feeding and receiving solutions must be changed when the equilibrium is reached. This is why pressure-driven studies with porous membranes are the closest to what can be found for industrial applications.

Pressure-driven filtrations using porous membranes are not abundant in the literature. In the case of Baudino et al., the set up was discontinuous: 15 mL of a feeding solution was packed into a dead-end column, pushed through the membrane with a nitrogen tank (5 bar) and the receiving solution was collected into a beaker (Fig. 11
.a) [175]. Zhu et al. reported a continuous filtration [168]. The feeding solution was loaded into a beaker, pumped through a plexiglass membrane containing the membrane, and rejected back into the beaker containing the feeding solution, with a certain flow rate (Fig. 11 .b). Finally, the tangential filtration described by Li et al. [176] was carried out by pumping the feed solution through 4 membrane cells. The membranes were placed so as to form a tubular geometry within the membrane cells. The concentrate (part of the feed that does not flow through the membrane) was flown towards the feed solution while the permeate (part of the feed that flows through the membranes) was recovered in a separate beaker, as shown in Fig. 11 .c.

With pressure-driven processes, an important characteristic is the permeance of the membranes, i.e., their capacity to admit a flow of matter. The membrane by Zhu et al. had the best water permeance of 580 L/m2/h (LMH) bar−1 [168], while other systems had permeances of 8.0 [176] and 14 LMH/bar [175]. This may be due to the use of Non-solvent Induced Phase Separation, for the preparation of Zhu's membrane, which provided the best membrane properties (notably in terms of higher porosity), while the system of Li et al. generated transport channels through the cavity of the 15C5s and Baudino's membrane had physical spacers created by the PVA. Regarding the adsorption properties, the system designed by Baudino et al. was evaluated by analyzing the stripping solution: once the feeding solution was passed through the membrane, an HCl stripping solution was also passed through, and analyzed. While 70 % of lithium was found in the stripping solution, 40 % of sodium was also found (with equimolar initial concentrations). In terms of permeation fluxes, we calculated values of 9.35 × 10−6 mg/cm2/s for lithium, and 2.31 × 10−5 mg/cm2/s for sodium, indicating a certain retention of lithium compared to sodium.

With similar pressure and temperature conditions (5 bar, 25 °C) and more difficult competitive conditions (50 times more Mg than Li), the system of Li et al. presented more promising results [176]. This is because the 15C5-bearing membrane did not sorb ions (as we have shown that it is too big to sorb Li correctly) but rather separated them according to their size, and only transported the ions that interacted with it. The authors explain that the 15C5s act like selective Li+ transport channels for mixtures of Li and Mg, which implied that the cavity was too large to enable the transport of Mg2+. Coupled with cross-flow filtration, this method greatly minimizes the clogging phenomenon. Conductivity measurements showed that 90 % of the magnesium was rejected back into the feeding solution while the lithium retention was −36.3 %, indicating that the concentration of lithium in the permeate (Cp, Li) is 1.36 times higher than in the feed (Cf, Li). In the end, the authors report a dynamic system allowing, in one hand, to obtain a concentrated lithium solution in the permeate, and a concentrated magnesium solution in the feed, i.e., good separation. In fact, Li et al. also performed three-stage NF processes to separate lithium from Salt Lake brine. Due to the concentrated brine, the osmotic pressure was quite high (3.3 bar) inducing a low permeability of the membrane (2.8 L/m2/h/bar) for the first stage. After the three NF stages, the Li+-concentrated solution contained 24 ppm of Li+ and 2.7 ppm of Mg2+. After a simple selective precipitation with Na2CO3, Li2CO3 was prepared from the three-stage NF ultimate permeate solution. Analyses showed that Li2CO3 was recovered with a purity of approximately 95 %, which is already sufficient for the reuse of lithium in electric vehicle LIB batteries for instance.

Finally, membranes present great adaptability for their implementation into membrane filtration processes. In order to reduce the ion separation time, pressure-driven configurations with NF or UF porous membranes are more convenient than osmosis. Combined with a crossflow set-up, this appears to be the most realistic configuration for continuous flow industrial recycling of lithium. More generally, different uses of the complexing functions approaches can be considered. On one hand, membranes can be functionalized to selectively adsorb Li: interesting retentions are obtained, but are based on a clogging mechanism, and require regeneration quite rapidly, which is not convenient for industrial applications. On the other hand, instead of adsorption, the transport of ions can be exploited with the use of cross-flow filtration: based on the size-fit principles of CEs, small ions can pass through the cavities of the CEs, while larger ions are rejected into the feed solution. This shows that the properties of CEs, and more generally complexing functions, can be exploited for purposes other than those originally intended. Nevertheless, membranes can always undergo several obstacles, such as fouling, upper solid limits or low mass transport due to low porosity or limited internal diffusion, which can be problematic for up-scaling.

Columns packed with lithium selective materials are also described in the literature, they are mainly studied at a laboratory scale. Table 10
summarizes the column sorption experiments that report the separation of lithium from aqueous solutions. Notably, Koo et al. designed polymer-supported oligo ethylene glycols that did not separate lithium and cobalt ions efficiently in a batch-wise depletion experiment [134]. Yet, they functionalized beads of Merrifield polyester-based resin with oligoethylene glycols to achieve separation of Li+ and Co2+ ions with a chromatography column display. The filtration of a solution containing 200 ppm of cobalt and lithium ions showed that cobalt ions have a better affinity with the resin, so Koo et al. exploited this to completely separate Li+ and Co2+ ions with a similar approach than that of Li et al. with a membrane [176]. Co2+ ions were complexed onto the resin, while Li+ ions were collected in the outlet solution. Secondly, Co2+ ions were stripped using a nitric acid solution. Chromatographically separating alkali metal ions reveals to be quite advantageous as resins can be washed and reused while remaining relatively low cost and quite affordable (in Koo's study, the resins were based on polymers that were purchased under 10 USD/L). According to the authors, the column method can be accelerated by putting sample solutions under pressure, and no further purification is necessary for the collected fractions. Although the oligoethylene glycol functions were not selective towards lithium, Koo's design was the only one to enable the full separation of lithium from competitive ions [134]. In Hayashita's study, 40 % of Li was recovered, and all the selectivity factors with Na, K, Rb and Cs were close to 1, indicating that as many ions were recovered together with the lithium [86]. Hankins et al. made the same observations with their functionalized silica gel, as the elution profile of 12C4 silica gel was similar to the elution profile of pristine silica gel, indicating that the 12C4 in this study was a weak binder of alkali metal cations [63]. X-ray diffraction analyses showed Na+ complexes may be more stable because the spacing of the ionizable side arm permits direct coordination between perching Na+ and the atomic oxygen of the carboxylate group. Finally, further investigations are necessary to improve the uptake of Li by column packed resins. Regarding elution time, columns may be seen as time-consuming. From the information found in the literature, feeding solutions can be eluted at a very small flow rate, such as 3.4 mL/min, involving >5 h of loading for one single liter of feeding solution [86]. Taking into consideration the upscaling of such a process, such rates can be seen as an important drawback.

The great adaptability of membrane materials has allowed for extensive research on the selective chemical adsorption of lithium. In-batch reactors, adsorption performances are reported to vary from 4.9 to 297 mg/g of polymer membrane, with particularly high sorption capacities for ion-imprinting membranes (27–34 mg/g) or chitosan-based membranes (169–297 mg/g). Notwithstanding, the work of Zhu et al. on chitosan membranes has certainly brought functionalized membranes into the forefront with an optimized process time, for low initial concentrations of lithium ions [168]. Moreover, membranes do not require the use of any supplementary organic solvents, which is an attractive feature from an environmental perspective (only a stripping solution is needed to recover lithium from the selective resin). Nevertheless, they can only be compared according to their global performances. Deeper mechanistic studies can be more challenging as sorption capacities are often given in mg of lithium complexed per gram of material and little information is given on the density of complexing units on the material (a matter quite recurrent in this review, and more generally in the literature). Additionally, the sorption conditions regularly differ from one article to another. Meanwhile, it would be interesting to further optimize this process for higher concentrations of lithium. And finally, the majority of studies conducted in batch reactors show interesting Li retention by adsorption, but this mechanism is likely to generate premature membrane fouling. In addition, a study in a dynamic system is necessary to also understand the dynamic retention of ions by the membrane, beyond sorption mechanisms. Concerning column separations, very little literature is available, and only one pilot-scale study is reported. Lithium-selective complexation functions do not seem quite effective in such a set-up, since the elution of all ions was similar whether the resins were functionalized or not.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Liquid-liquid extraction for the selective recovery of lithium
ce:section.ce:para:
Another common way of implementing complexation agents is to use them as hydrophobic materials. In this case, the complexation agents, which are used in their molecular state or grafted onto a hydrophobic polymer, are called extractants and are dissolved in an organic carrier solvent, called diluent. This diluent is non-miscible in water (typically dichloromethane, DCM) and can be brought into contact with an aqueous solution containing lithium ions and competitive species, to perform liquid-liquid extraction (LLE). In order to create a high contact surface between the complexation agents and ions, a great amount of energy is necessary (i.e., quick stirring action resulting from vortex agitation, magnetic stirring or vigorous mechanical shaking). After stirring, the following steps consist in centrifugation/decantation of the solution, separation of enriched organic phase (extract) from the deficient aqueous phase (raffinate) and separation of lithium from the complexation functions. One of the most important parameters in LLEs is the organic/water phase ratio (O/A) as it can greatly influence the interactions between the extractant and ions. For further information on LLE technique and basics, Schmidt et al. reported an in-depth review on this topic [177]. Preliminary lab-scale experimental LLEs for the extraction of lithium with organic complexing groups from feed solutions are described, and unfortunately, no literature was found concerning the upscaling of this method to pilot processes. The preliminary results are shown in Table 11
.

Typically, CEs are rather hydrophobic, so using them in their molecular state directly into the aqueous solution can be very difficult, yet they are widely described in LLEs. The choice of the solvent has an inevitable influence on the thermodynamics of alkali metals complexation with CEs. From a theoretical point of view, Boda et al. describe that the metal/CE binding energy can vary depending on the solvent, and notably its polarity [178]. The polarity of a solvent is defined by its dielectric constant. The authors noted that the higher the dielectric constant is, the higher the interaction energy becomes and so the better the solvent is for the extraction. In consequence, high polarity solvents are more suitable for optimized extraction of lithium.

According to Table 11 , DCM and DCE are regularly used, in a 1:1 ratio with the aqueous solution. However, one of the disadvantages of using LLEs is the use of toxic, high Volatile Organic Component (VOC) and flammable organic solvents. To solve these challenges and provide environmentally friendly solutions, researchers have focused their work on ionic liquids for LLEs and the results are quite promising. Ionic liquids are charged systems that are in the liquid state at room temperature. They have a great potential for a wide range of applications due to their excellent physico-chemical properties, notably as a greener alternative to conventional organic solvents, in electro-chemistry as electrolytes, in analytical chemistry for chromatography [179], for instance and additionally for the solvatometallurgical processing of metal oxides [180]. They can bear long alkyl chains to provide hydrophobic properties and act as an innovative environmentally friendly replacement to LLE solvents.

Shi et al. studied the effect of O/A ratio on the sorption and it was found that the extraction efficiency increased with the increasing of the O/A phase ratio. This is more than likely due to the increased probability of collision between organic molecule and lithium ions with an increase of O/A ratio. Overall, the molecular systems used in LLEs for the recovery of lithium were almost all quite efficient and selective according to Table 11 . Nevertheless, Li et al. discuss a few drawbacks of LLEs with their system, such as the loss of complexing system in the aqueous phase, the generation of third phase, and difficult separation of organic/aqueous phase during LLE [160]. For these reasons, the authors report the embedment of crowned spiropyran into PVDF materials for the preparation of porous microsphere as an adsorbent to recover lithium ions. Additionally, Sun et al. report two main issues with the reported preliminary study. First, the single extraction rate of lithium was quite insignificant (only 37 % ~ 1 mg/gpolymer), but could be improved by multi-stage solvent extraction. Secondly, the solubility of this CE was insufficient, therefore it could not be used in higher concentrations of lithium, i.e., concentrations of real-life LIB leachates. Further work should report the modification of B15C5 with a side chain alkylation reaction to increase solubility in organic solvent.

Grafting complexation agents onto polymers provides a higher complex stability compared to free agents as they are linked to the polymer and therefore exhibit a decreased entropy loss [58]. In their work, Oral et al. compared free 9C3 agents to polymer-supported 9C3 in similar conditions, and showed that the complex stability constant of the polymer system was almost 104 times higher than for the free agent. More particularly, they designed PS-b-PMMA copolymers containing 9C3s. The PMMA block is functionalized to complex lithium, whereas the PS block is designed to increase separation from the aqueous phase during centrifugation as it is a hydrophobic polymer. Such a system allowed for a lithium uptake of 39 % of initial Li+ ions, which corresponds to 0.03 mol/mol CE groups or 0.21 g/mol CE. This result was observed with the PS52-b-P(MAA12-r-CE3MA36)76 polymer, where the weight percentage of 9C3 functionalized methacrylate was 36 wt% in a polymer of 76 kDa. This information along with the molar mass of CE3MA (278 g/mol) allowed us to calculate the number of CEs in one mole of polymer (98 mol CE per mol of polymer) and the mass of polymer corresponding to one mole of CE, which is 760 g polymer/mol of CE. In the end, the 0.21 g/mol CE corresponds to a lithium uptake of 0.27 mg/g polymer, which was in the lower range of the sorption performances.

To conclude, the use of complexation agents in their molecular state for LLEs presents numerous drawbacks that are described above. Notably, several systems present insufficient hydrophobic properties allowing for loss of complexation agent during the process. To improve hydrophobicity, long alkyl chains can be grafted onto the complexation agent, or the latter can be directly grafted onto a hydrophobic polymer system. The performance of molecular systems used in LLEs range from 0.073 to 9.7 mg/g, while the only case of polymer-based LLE reports an extraction of 0.27 mg/g of polymer. Unfortunately, polymers for LLE of lithium are not abundantly reported in the literature, yet they present great potential for Li/CE systems, which still needs to be explored. Nevertheless, a major drawback of LLEs is the use of toxic, high VOC solvents. Ionic liquids are great candidates for the replacement of organic solvents, but another solution could be to completely remove the organic solvent from the process and work with water-soluble polymers as described in the following part.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Single-phase separation process for the recovery of lithium: future valuable prospects
ce:section.ce:para:
In this final part, the use of complexation agents in a single-phase design is described. Here, the ion-selective system (molecular or polymeric state) is water-soluble and directly placed into the aqueous sample containing the mixture of ions (Table 12
). A supplementary separation step is necessary to separate the sorbent from the aqueous solution. This can be done with a polymer enhanced ultrafiltration (PEUF) setup, which consists in a pressure-driven separation technique combining high molecular weight and water-soluble materials, retained by ultrafiltration (UF) membranes [181]. Depending on the molecular weight, nano, or microfiltration can be employed. PEUF processes have been employed for different metal ions recovery such as Cu and Cd with poly(vinyl sulfonic acid) and sodium poly(styrene sulfonate) [182], Cu with poly(sodium acrylate) [183], Zn, Cu and Cd with polyacrylic acid [184], Cr with polyethyleneimine [185], etc. However, to our knowledge, the use of PEUF has only been described once for the recovery of lithium. Oyarce et al. report the use of poly(potassium sulfopropylacrylate) (PSPAK), a water-soluble polymer with sulfonate groups, to recover lithium in a PEUF process [144]. An 80 % Li removal was observed in optimal conditions, however in the presence of a competitive divalent ion Mg2+, the removal efficiency decreased to 20 %: the divalent ion had a higher affinity for the polymer since it was more electropositive, causing a higher electrostatic association with the sulfonate groups. This was confirmed with competitive sorption with Na+ and K+, where the Li removal efficiency did not decrease as much (40 %). The PEUF process can present several advantages, as it does not cause a significant fouling effect on the membrane as the sorption of the metals occur on the polymer on the UF membrane, and not inside of it. However, the filtration process could be optimized with the use of stimuli-sensitive systems, and more particularly polymers that could observe a change of solubility in an aqueous phase under the effect of a stimulus (pH, temperature, irradiation, etc.). After sorption, the polymers could be precipitated and, a last separation step (filtration, sedimentation, etc.) could be undergone to recover the Li+/polymer complex. In the case where membrane separation is applied, the precipitated state of the polymer generally could allow for a higher and therefore less energy-efficient membrane cut-off threshold. Although such systems have not been reported for the recovery of lithium to our knowledge, this final separation process presents great advantages.

Notably, temperature-responsive (or thermoresponsive) polymers are widely studied in the polymer field. They are known to exhibit a lower critical solution temperature (LCST), which consists of a critical temperature above which a polymer becomes insoluble. Typical thermo-responsive polymers are poly(acrylamide)s, poly(methacrylamide)s, poly(N-vinylamide)s, poly(oxazoline)s, poly(oxide)s, poly(vinylether)s. These polymers are composed of a balance between a hydrophilic part and a hydrophobic part. Generally, the longer the hydrophobic part is, the lower the LCST is, as less energy is necessary for the system to become hydrophobic and precipitate. For instance, poly(N-ethylacrylamide) has an LCST between 72 °C and 85.5 °C whereas adding one carbon to the chain (poly(N-propylacrylamide)) lowers the LCST to approximately 25 °C. The LCST can also depend on the molar mass of the polymer in some cases, for example, poly(2-ethyl-2-oxazoline) has an LCST above 100 °C for Mn < 10,000 g/mol, while molar masses above 20,000 g/mol generate an LCST between 60 and 78 °C [186]. On the contrary, in the case of poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) the LCST is barely dependent of the molar mass [187]. Thermosensitive polymers can be employed in the selective complexation of lithium in their soluble state, and then be separated, after an increase in temperature, in their solid state. It is important to note that when the polymeric system is a covalently crosslinked network (thermoset), it is no longer soluble in water: instead, the network can swell in water. In this case, the critical temperature is called Volume Phase Transition Temperature (VPTT). Under the VPTT, the network has a better affinity with water and will swell, while above the VPTT, it will expulse the water and shrink. Kim et al. report the use of thermoresponsive 12C4-containing crosslinked hydrogels that present a VPTT [66], Park et al. report the use of thermoresponsive functionalized alginate [162,188]. These thermoresponsive networks are typically exploited for their crosslinked properties: the network can sorb Li+ in their swelled form as water and ion transport through the material is enhanced, and Li+ can be released when the network is shrunk at higher temperatures. For instance, with the hydrogel designed by Kim et al., a 89 % adsorption efficiency is observed at room temperature. The hydrogels are in a swollen state and the CEs have the space to easily and selectively host lithium ions. After removing the hydrogels from the solution, redispersing them in double-distilled deionized water and heating above the VPTT, opposing interactions are observed. The polymer chains prefer to interact with themselves rather than with the solvent, inducing a shrinkage of the material. Meanwhile, lithium-containing CEs will tend to remain in their “expanded” configuration since the hosted cation interacting with its oxygen atoms prevents it from changing configurations. Since 69 % of the sorbed lithium was released, the interactions causing the shrinking of the hydrogel would appear stronger than the interactions maintaining the Li/CE complexes, triggering the expulsion of lithium ions.

This ion expulsion phenomenon is not observed in linear water-soluble polymers (thermoplastics) since increasing the temperature above the LCST after sorption does not cause the ions to desorb. Our group recently reported the synthesis of a thermoresponsive copolymer bearing B12C4s and composed of NiPAAm [76]. The copolymer presented sorption properties in the lower range of properties obtained with other 12C4 systems: this could be attributed to the presence of the benzo group as mentioned in Part III.1.1. While improving the sorption properties of our material is an ongoing work, the thermoresponsive properties of the already synthesized copolymer have shown great processability, as the polymer precipitated at temperatures above 25 °C, forming a gum-size ball in the medium, while the small proportion of ions that were sorbed at 17 °C (T < LCST) was not released at 35 °C (T > LCST).

To our knowledge, this recent work was the first to report the use of stimuli-responsive linear polymers for the selective recovery of lithium from aqueous solutions. Meanwhile, other examples regarding the recovery of other ions have been reported and illustrate the usefulness of such systems. Ji et al. described the exact use of such water-soluble polymers by preparing thermo-responsive polymers with pendent calix[4]pyrrole receptors for the recovery of cesium in aqueous solutions [189]. The polymer is firstly soluble in water, allowing for great surface interactions between the polymer and aqueous solution. This is followed by a change in solubility (T > TLCST with LCST = 34.8 °C) for the extraction by filtration of the polymer. The best extraction percentage of cesium in this study, after heating (50 °C) and filtration, was 62.5 %. This strategy is thus considered to have great potential for the development of new water purification processes. Graillot et al. also describe the use of functionalized thermosensitive copolymers, for the removal of nickel ions from aqueous solutions [190]. The copolymer was composed of a comonomer that provides thermosensitive properties while the other comonomer was functionalized with a Ni-selective phosphonated groups. An in-depth study of the influence of temperature on the sorption capacity of the polymer was reported. In the solubility domain of the polymer, at a temperature lower than the LCST, the polymer chains are dispersed in the solution and each chain sorbs independently. At temperatures around the LCST, the polymer chains move closer to each other. The phosphonated moieties are more hydrophilic than the thermosensitive moieties, meaning the particles assemble with a thermosensitive-rich core and a shell rich in phosphonated moieties. Consequently, the complexing groups are closer one to each other on the surface of the particle, which facilitates the interaction with metallic ions, i.e., improving the sorption efficiency of the material. Finally, at temperatures above the LCST, polymer-polymer interactions are preferred, meaning the particles shrink, inducing water expulsion and reducing the number of complexing groups available, i.e., a lower adsorption capacity. In the end, using a thermoresponsive polymer allowed the authors to improve the sorption of metals in the soluble state, but also to consider conventional filtration (less energy-consuming) instead of ultrafiltration for the membrane separation [191]. Nevertheless, it would be very interesting to carry out more investigations on the use of thermo-responsive thermoplastics such as materials described by Ji or Graillot for the recovery of lithium ions. The association of lithium-selective functions with the single-phase separation process using thermo-responsive thermoplastics could present great potential for the process of recovering lithium from LIB leachates, since complexation was proven to be more effective with a soluble material, and the physical extraction can be undergone by a simple increase in temperature and lower energy filtration. In the end, compared to the insoluble supports described above, the single-phase separation process allows the use of water-soluble materials, which provides a more homogenous interaction between the ions in the solution and the sorbing functions [165]. This method requires no addition of organic solvent, it enables unlimited diffusion of ions through the material and towards the complexing functions, due to the solubility of the support, and can be performed at room temperature with low energy consumption. It is then necessary to separate the soluble polymer from the aqueous solution by using a membrane separation process and/or modifying the solubility of the material, with thermosensitive polymers for instance. Investigating the use of simple polymers (polymers obtained from the free radical polymerization of commercially available monomers for instance) with sulfonic or carboxylic acids, for the separation of lithium in the context of LIB recycling could be of great interest. The work of Oyarce et al. has shown that acidic functions can potentially show selectivity towards divalent ions over lithium, and LIB leachates are composed of lithium, and divalent ions. Consequently, the PEUF process could be used to obtain lithium-concentrated permeates. Parallelly, exploiting the thermoresponsive properties of linear polymers and coupling them with selective complexing groups can allow for the use of less energy-consuming filtration processes for the separation of the sorbent from the aqueous solution. To our knowledge, only our work has been published on the recovery of lithium with thermoresponsive polymers in the context of LIB recycling and opens the way to new processes development.

To resume, implementing lithium-selective complexing groups into recycling processes is widely described in the literature. These methods along with their benefits and drawbacks are summarized in Table 13
. It is noteworthy to mention that many articles report the use of the same complexing groups, and yet the selectivity and efficiency towards lithium can vary a lot. This can be due to a difference in feeding solution parameters, but it can also come from the nature of the polymer support, whose influence has not yet been fully identified. What we know for sure is that using an insoluble or soluble polymer support allows for much better control, effectiveness and reusability than using complexation agents in their molecular state. Their use in their molecular state, notably in LLEs, presents several drawbacks such as the loss of the complexing groups in the aqueous phase, the generation of third phase, and difficult phase separation.

While immobile solid supports allow for the easiest handling, as well as low energy consumption, they may encounter fouling, upper solids limits or low mass transport. These drawbacks can be avoided with mobile solid supports. These supports do not require sophisticated setups; however, a separation step is necessary to remove the supports from the feeding solution. In terms of complexing function availability, mobile solid supports combined with porous or fibrous configurations provide better diffusion of ions towards the functions. However, using soluble polymers was proven to maximize the availability of the complexing agents since the polymer supports are in a more relaxed state, meaning ions have to diffuse less through the polymer to access the functions. Although this hasn't been proven for lithium sorption, it has been observed for other metals with several polymer systems. In the case of liquid-liquid extractions, the contact between the ions in the aqueous phase and the complexing agents in the organic phase must be increased through high agitation. Yet LLEs encounter a few drawbacks described earlier. Finally, using water-soluble polymer supports allows for the best diffusion of ions towards the complexing functions as they are both in the same phase as the ions.

A range of sorption capacities per process is reported in Table 13. It is important to note that the majority of published work on the selective sorption of Li is limited to laboratory-scale experiments, with in-batch characterizations. Only recently have membranes begun being studied in depth with pilot-scale processes. The performances naturally depend on the use of the complexation agent and their density in the material, but these figures still indicate the effectiveness of each material and method. Nevertheless, water-soluble polymers possess great potential for the recovery of lithium in aqueous solutions and further research on this topic would be of great interest.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Desorption of lithium and reusability of the extractants
ce:section.ce:para: What has not been mentioned in this review yet is the desorption step, which follows the sorption of lithium onto solid extractants, or the extraction of the Li+-containing polymers from an aqueous or organic phase. Table 14 summarizes the methods to desorb lithium from materials, as well as the performance of the materials after a certain time of reuse. Generally, lithium ions are stripped with strong acidic solutions such as HCl, H2SO4, HNO3 with concentrations ranging from 10 mM [26] to 3 M [139]. Mechanistically, hydrogen atoms that are present in acidic stripping solutions have great affinity with lithium-selective complexing agents. These complexing groups are often composed of electro-donating oxygen or nitrogen atoms to which hydrogen bonds can easily be formed. In this way, available acidic H+ switch places with weaker-interacting Li+ ions, allowing the release of Li+ ions in the stripping solution. In fact, this observation had already been made earlier for the optimization of sorption procedure, as higher pH is preferable to avoid competition with H+ ions. The concentration of the stripping solution depends on the amount of Li+ ions that is to be desorbed. For instance, Hashemi et al. determined that nitric acid with a minimum concentration of 0.5 M was necessary to fully recover 100 % of the Li+ contained in the polymer system. With more diluted solutions, the protonation of donating hetero-atoms of the binding sites was incomplete. It is noteworthy to observe that 12C4 and 14C4 ethers present similar results with regards to desorption of lithium, indicating that the stronger stability of Li/14C4 complexes presented in Part III.1. does not affect the reversibility of the complexation. This widely used stripping procedure produces acidic wastewater, sometimes in large quantities, which is not optimal for environmental reasons. That's why some researchers study greener alternatives to avoid such inconveniences. For instance, the photochromic spirobenzopyran-based materials which could complex lithium in the “open” irradiated form could desorb lithium by simply removing the material from irradiation and forming the “closed” form [79]. Warshawsky et al. wrote that complexation of ions onto CEs could be reversible by temperature increase as salt-coordination mechanisms were temperature-sensitive process [192]. Yet to our knowledge no article in the literature reports the use of thermo-sensitivity of Li+/CE complexes to reverse the complexation. Instead, some researchers focused on the thermo-sensitivity of crosslinked polymer supports for the green release of Li. This is the case for crosslinked N-isopropylacrylamide hydrogel designed by Kim et al. [66] or alginate-based systems synthesized by Park et al. [161,162,188]. As mentioned earlier, for temperatures above the LCST, crosslinked networks would shrink and expulse water since polymer-polymer interactions are preferred. Based on this phenomenon, Li were also effectively expulsed from the material, while no acidic solution was necessary.

Unfortunately, these green alternatives have not been investigated enough and studies reporting their use are not abundant in the literature. Luckily, the acidic washing solutions do not seem to severely damage any of the reported polymer systems described above. After the desorption of lithium and the recovery of the sorbent, several studies focus on the reuse of the sorbent for a certain number of cycles and evaluate their effectiveness after x reuses. This a very important feature since single-use polymers are very unpopular and unwanted nowadays, yet it would be an attractive asset if they were reusable and still efficient after several cycles of use. In all-the-above articles, several sorption-desorption experiments were carried out. The authors usually report up to ten cycles after which the sorbent exhibits a sorption capacity of above 90 % of the sorption capacity of the first cycle. Some articles report a lower efficiency (70–85 % of the initial sorption capacity), after only five cycles, however the authors remain quite optimistic with regards to the stability and reusability of their designs [73,89,123,132]. The difference in efficiency may be linked to the loss of complexing groups during LLEs [89,123], or more generally to the use of free or only physically bonded complexing groups [73] that can be lost during repeated cycles, causing a decrease in efficiency. Lastly, ageing of sorption systems is rarely mentioned in the literature. The ageing of a material can result from high-temperature exposure, acidic conditions, loss of sorption agents over time, etc. For the latter, the risk of complexing group loss is quite inevitable when they are in their molecular state. Limjuco et al. carried out SEM images of their used polymer microfibers which had excellent physical stability since no CEs were lost during adsorption-desorption operations [87]. This highlights the importance of covalently attaching CEs to a polymeric support. This key factor could help improve the long-term efficiency of systems in which the complexing groups are not covalently bonded [73].

Although LIB recycling processes are currently industrialized, many challenges have yet to be solved. For instance, the dismantling step is still mostly done by hand and poses threats to human workers handling hazardous and flammable components. It is a current challenge to identify more secure and automated ways to perform this step with the same efficiency and precision, and also considering the great variety of battery composition, design and assembly. Notably, the leachates described in Table 2 present Li/Co molar ratios varying from 0.7 to 350 illustrating this wide variety. This is in fact another issue relative to the studies described in this review: the extractants must be versatile enough to ensure consistent effective separation in any leachate composition, despite the many compositions. Several of the above-mentioned studies have worked on only one single multi-component solution, but would their study present the same results in a similar solution with different concentrations? This also applies to the selected process which must also be adaptable to the leachate variety, in an industrial context. Some of the reported studies would require further investigations for in-depth comprehension and evaluation of the proposed designs. For instance, most of the complexing agents described above complex lithium selectively but not exclusively as other ions are also partially complexed. More than half of the reported membrane designs have not been implemented into a pilot-scale experiment, which membrane technology is actually intended for.

Finally, with the growing interest in greener and more sustainable chemistry, further investigations on the use of biobased, biodegradable and/or non-toxic materials is of interest. This remains a great challenge for industrials as sustainable chemistry comes with a higher price, and ensuring similar efficiency is not always guaranteed when replacing petroleum-based/highly reactive chemicals. In this sense, in-depth studies of the ageing of proposed materials would be an interesting indicator to estimate the economic viability of such designs.

This review aims at regrouping all of the most common and some few original ways of selectively and efficiently recovering lithium from aqueous solutions. More particularly, we wished to focus on lithium that originated from wasted LIB leachates for environmental and economic reasons. Leaching processes of wasted LIBs have been abundantly studied in the past decades. They are usually undergone with sulfuric, nitric or hydrochloric acid, in combination with a reducing agent. A typical average LIB leachate composition was extracted from the various compositions found in the literature: lithium, cobalt, nickel, manganese, iron, aluminum, copper with respective median concentrations of 1.18, 7.79, 1.68, 2.04, 0.24, 1.80 and 2.54 g/L. It is important to keep in mind that, depending on the nature of the batteries and the pre-treatments of the recycling process, iron, copper and aluminum can easily be removed from this composition. Meanwhile, separating lithium, cobalt, nickel and manganese is the greatest challenge in LIB recycling. More particularly, this review focused on the separation of lithium from the other ions. If plenty of studies have shed light on optimizing the recovery of lithium from natural aqueous solutions, research must now focus on greener alternatives for the recovery of lithium from the leaching solutions issued from the recycling of wasted batteries as this is where the majority of available lithium on earth is headed. Crown ethers and acid groups (sulfonic, carboxylic) seem to be interesting candidates for the stable and selective complexation of lithium, through host-guest complexation or simple electrostatic interactions. The processing of lithium-selective sorbents can take many forms such as mobile solid support extractions, membrane filtration, column concentration, liquid-liquid extractions with polymers that are soluble in organic solvents or single-phase extractions with water-soluble supports. Each of these processes exhibit great benefits as well as unfortunate drawbacks. More particularly, using water-soluble polymers has rarely been described for Li recovery, yet this method could drastically enhance the interactions between lithium and sorption agents compared to membrane or mobile supports as well as its processability in a PEUF system for instance. Finally, reusability is almost systematically reported for organic extractants, which present efficient and stable regeneration after acidic exposure.

Ariella F. Liberati: Writing – original draft. Benedicte Prelot: Writing – review & editing. Jean-Pierre Mericq: Writing – review & editing. Catherine Faur: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Sophie Monge: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft.
